exercises.title: LEGB Lookup Order
exercises.description: Python looks up a name in this order: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in. Trace the LEGB rule by printing x from different scopes.
exercises.starterCode:
x = "global"

def outer():
    x = "enclosing"
    def inner():
        x = "local"
        print(x)    # finds local first
    inner()
    print(x)        # finds enclosing

outer()
print(x)            # finds global
hints: Each function creates its own local scope.
Python searches L → E → G → B and uses the first match found.

exercises.title: global Keyword
exercises.description: The global keyword lets a function modify a global variable. Without it, assignment inside a function creates a new local variable.
exercises.starterCode:
count = 0

def increment():
    global count    # Declare we want to modify the global
    count += 1

def reset():
    global count
    count = 0

increment()
increment()
increment()
print(f"After 3 increments: {count}")

reset()
print(f"After reset: {count}")
hints: Without 'global count', Python would try to create a local count inside increment().
global count tells Python: 'use the global count, not a local one'.

exercises.title: Closure: Function That Remembers Its Enclosing Scope
exercises.description: A closure is an inner function that captures variables from its enclosing function. The captured values persist after the outer function returns.
exercises.starterCode:
def make_multiplier(factor):
    """Return a function that multiplies its argument by factor."""
    def multiply(n):
        return n * factor   # 'factor' from enclosing scope
    return multiply

# Create two different multiplier functions
double = make_multiplier(2)
triple = make_multiplier(3)

print(double(5))
print(triple(5))
print(double(10))
print(triple(10))
hints: make_multiplier(2) creates a 'double' function that captures factor=2.
Each call to make_multiplier creates a new closure with its own factor value.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Local vs Global Scope
warmup.description: Variables defined inside a function are local; they don't affect the global scope.
warmup.starterCode:
value = 100   # global

def modify():
    value = 999   # local — does NOT change global value
    print(f"Inside function: {value}")

modify()
print(f"Outside function: {value}")
